questions.question: Define method overloading in Java and explain the two primary ways to achieve it.
keyPoints: Method overloading allows multiple methods in the same class to share the same name but have different parameter lists.
Overloading by changing the number of parameters (e.g., `calculate(int a)` vs `calculate(int a, int b)`).
Overloading by changing the type of parameters (e.g., `calculate(int a)` vs `calculate(double a)`).

questions.question: Explain why method overloading is a form of compile-time polymorphism. Include a discussion on how the correct overloaded method is selected and why return type alone is insufficient for overloading.
keyPoints: The specific method to call is determined at compile time by the compiler based on the method signature (name and parameter list), hence it is 'compile-time polymorphism'.
The selection is made by matching the number, types, and order of the arguments in the method call to the available parameter lists of the overloaded methods.

Return type is not part of the method signature. Methods cannot be overloaded by just changing the return type because it would create ambiguity for the compiler when a method call is made without using its return value.

questions.question: A common pitfall in method overloading involves methods with parameters that are in the same inheritance hierarchy (e.g., `Object`, `String`). Using a concrete code example, explain how the compiler resolves an overloaded method call in such a scenario and the potential for unexpected behavior at runtime if not carefully designed.
keyPoints: The compiler always chooses the most specific method available that matches the method call arguments. For example, given `print(String str)` and `print(Object obj)`, a call with a String argument will bind to `print(String str)` as String is more specific than Object.
If an exact match is not found, the compiler will use implicit casting (widening) to find a compatible method, but it will not narrow without an explicit cast.

This can lead to unexpected behavior if a new, more specific overloaded method is added to a class later, changing which method existing client code calls (e.g., a method call that previously used `print(Object obj)` might now use a new `print(String str)` method, altering program output). This highlights that overloading is resolved at compile-time, not based on the actual runtime type of the object.
